modules.title: Module 1: Hard Disk Drives (HDDs): Fundamentals & Performance Analysis
modules.goal: Understand the physical structure, operational principles, and performance characteristics of Hard Disk Drives, and be able to calculate total I/O access time.

lessons.title: Lesson 1.1: HDD Physical Structure & Basic Operation
lessons.objective: Describe the internal components of an HDD and explain how data is physically stored and accessed, identifying potential failure points.

activities.prompt: Review slides 4, 5, 6 (sections on physical structure, platters, heads, rotation) and slide 15 (sections on sectors, tracks, cylinders).
activities.expected_outcome: Students should be able to identify and describe the main physical components of an HDD and how they interact.

activities.prompt: Review the lecture segment from [04:26 – 08:28] focusing on the description of physical components and the 'cylinder' concept.
activities.expected_outcome: Students will reinforce their understanding of HDD physical layout and its operational advantages.

activities.prompt: Draw and label the key components of an HDD (platters, spindle, arm, read/write heads) and briefly explain the function of each. Explain the concept of a 'cylinder' in your own words.
activities.expected_outcome: Students can visually represent and articulate the functions of HDD parts and the cylinder concept.

mini_quiz: What is the primary function of the arm assembly in an HDD?
Explain the concept of a 'cylinder' in HDD storage and its benefit.
What is a 'head crash' and what causes it?

lessons.title: Lesson 1.2: HDD Performance Metrics & I/O Time Calculation
lessons.objective: Define and differentiate between seek time, rotational latency, and transfer rate, and calculate the total average I/O time for an HDD given specific parameters.

activities.prompt: Review slides 4 (definitions of delays), slide 6 (performance numbers, latency calculation), and slide 7 (I/O time formula and example).
activities.expected_outcome: Students will understand the definitions and formulas for different HDD performance metrics.

activities.prompt: Review the lecture segment from [05:52 – 13:15] for definitions and general calculation principles, and from [13:15 – 24:37] for the detailed numerical example.
activities.expected_outcome: Students will see a practical application of the I/O time calculation and understand each component.

activities.prompt: Work through the example calculation from the lecture/slides independently. Then, solve a similar problem: Calculate the total I/O time for transferring a 8KB block on a 10,000 RPM disk with a 4ms average seek time, a 1.2Gb/s transfer rate, and a 0.15ms controller overhead.
activities.expected_outcome: Students will be able to apply the I/O time formula and perform calculations accurately, demonstrating LO1.

mini_quiz: List the four main components that contribute to total I/O time in an HDD.
How is average rotational latency calculated from RPM?
Given an average seek time of 8ms, a 7200 RPM disk, a 1Gb/s transfer rate, and a 0.2ms controller overhead, what is the approximate average rotational latency (in ms)?

modules.title: Module 2: Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) Technologies
modules.goal: Understand the characteristics, advantages, disadvantages, and management challenges of Non-Volatile Memory devices, including SSDs.

lessons.title: Lesson 2.1: SSDs and NVM Characteristics
lessons.objective: Compare and contrast SSDs with HDDs based on reliability, cost, lifespan, capacity, and performance, and identify different forms of NVM.

activities.prompt: Read slide 9 which provides an overview of NVM devices, their types, and comparative pros and cons against HDDs.
activities.expected_outcome: Students will identify various NVM types and understand their key characteristics relative to HDDs.

activities.prompt: Watch a short educational video comparing SSDs and HDDs (e.g., search 'SSD vs HDD explained').
activities.expected_outcome: Students will gain a visual and practical understanding of the differences in performance and application.

mini_quiz: List two advantages of SSDs over HDDs and two disadvantages.
What does 'DWPD' stand for and what does it measure?
Why do SSDs not have seek time or rotational latency?

lessons.title: Lesson 2.2: NVM Management Challenges & Algorithms
lessons.objective: Explain the unique challenges in managing NVM (e.g., page/block write operations, erase limits) and describe algorithms like Flash Translation Layer (FTL), garbage collection, and wear leveling.

activities.prompt: Read slide 10 (NVM write challenges, lifespan) and slide 11 (NAND Flash Controller Algorithms like FTL, garbage collection, wear leveling).
activities.expected_outcome: Students will grasp the technical complexities of NVM write operations and the solutions implemented.

activities.prompt: In your own words, explain how wear leveling helps extend the operational lifespan of an SSD.
activities.expected_outcome: Students can articulate the importance and mechanism of wear leveling.

mini_quiz: Why can't pages in NVM be overwritten in place?
What is the primary purpose of the Flash Translation Layer (FTL)?
Briefly describe 'garbage collection' in the context of NVM.

lessons.title: Lesson 2.3: Volatile Memory as Mass Storage
lessons.objective: Understand the concept and use cases of RAM drives (DRAM used as mass-storage devices).

activities.prompt: Read slide 12 covering DRAM as a mass-storage device, including RAM drives, and their applications.
activities.expected_outcome: Students will understand how volatile memory can function as temporary mass storage and its advantages.

mini_quiz: Why are RAM drives not considered true secondary storage devices?
What is a primary use case for RAM drives in a computing system?

modules.title: Module 3: Storage System Management & Scheduling
modules.goal: Analyze various I/O scheduling algorithms for HDDs and NVM, and understand key aspects of storage device management including error handling and data organization.

lessons.title: Lesson 3.1: Disk Attachment & Address Mapping
lessons.objective: Identify common bus interfaces for storage devices and explain how logical addresses are mapped to physical locations on a disk.

activities.prompt: Read slide 14 on host-attached storage, I/O buses (SATA, SAS, USB, FC, NVMe), and controllers. Also, read slide 15 on address mapping, logical blocks, sectors, tracks, and cylinders.
activities.expected_outcome: Students will recognize different storage interfaces and grasp the logical-to-physical address translation process.

mini_quiz: What is NVMe and why is it important for NVM devices?
How are disk drives typically addressed as 'logical blocks'?
What is the significance of 'low-level formatting'?

lessons.title: Lesson 3.2: Hard Disk Drive (HDD) Scheduling Algorithms
lessons.objective: Explain the purpose of disk scheduling and describe the operation of common algorithms like FCFS, SCAN, and C-SCAN, evaluating their performance trade-offs.

activities.prompt: Read slides 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23 which introduce HDD scheduling and detail the FCFS, SCAN, and C-SCAN algorithms with illustrations.
activities.expected_outcome: Students will understand the necessity of disk scheduling and the mechanics of basic algorithms.

activities.prompt: Search for and watch external educational videos demonstrating disk scheduling algorithms (e.g., FCFS, SCAN, C-SCAN) with animated head movements.
activities.expected_outcome: Visual aids will solidify understanding of how each algorithm processes requests.

activities.prompt: Given a queue of I/O requests (e.g., 98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67) and a starting head position (e.g., 53) on a disk with 0-199 cylinders, simulate FCFS, SCAN (moving towards 199 first), and C-SCAN and calculate the total head movement for each.
activities.expected_outcome: Students can apply scheduling algorithms and calculate head movement, demonstrating LO3.

mini_quiz: What is the primary goal of disk scheduling in an operating system?
Describe the key difference between the SCAN and C-SCAN algorithms.
Why might FCFS not be an optimal choice for disk scheduling in a busy system?

lessons.title: Lesson 3.3: Advanced Scheduling, NVM Scheduling & Error Handling
lessons.objective: Understand advanced scheduling concepts (e.g., deadline scheduler), specific considerations for NVM scheduling, and principles of error detection and correction in storage.

activities.prompt: Read slide 24 (advanced HDD scheduling, deadline scheduler), slide 25 (NVM scheduling considerations, IOPS, write amplification), and slide 26 (error detection and correction codes).
activities.expected_outcome: Students will learn about more sophisticated scheduling techniques and how data integrity is maintained.

mini_quiz: How does Linux's 'deadline' scheduler aim to improve performance and prevent starvation?
What is 'write amplification' in NVM, and what impact does it have on performance and lifespan?
Differentiate between error detection and error correction codes, providing an example of each.

lessons.title: Lesson 3.4: Storage Device Management & Swap Space
lessons.objective: Explain concepts like low-level and logical formatting, disk partitioning, and the role of swap space management.

activities.prompt: Read slide 27 (Storage Device Management: formatting, partitioning). Refer to the textbook's relevant section on 'Swap-Space Management' (mentioned in lecture at [03:10 – 03:15] and slide 2).
activities.expected_outcome: Students will understand how storage devices are prepared for use by the operating system and the function of swap space.

mini_quiz: What is the fundamental difference between low-level (physical) formatting and logical formatting?
What is the primary purpose of swap space in an operating system?
How does disk partitioning affect how a disk is utilized?

resources: Operating System Concepts – 10th Edition, Chapter 11: Mass-Storage Systems

emphases.statement: Even though Solid State Drives (SSDs) are increasingly common, understanding Hard Disk Drives (HDDs) remains vital due to their continued use in specific applications like data centers and AI infrastructure.
emphases.why: This emphasis sets the context for the chapter, explaining the relevance of studying HDDs despite newer technologies, and justifies the time spent on this 'easiest chapter'. It's crucial for understanding modern computer resource usage (LO1, LO3).
emphases.evidence: The teacher explicitly states the declining but re-emerging importance of HDDs for data centers. Slides list 'Overview of Mass Storage Structure' which includes HDDs.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: the importance of hard disk is just going down for quite some time now because of the oscillic states. Yet we do have hard disk again gaining up more for data centers... So we really need to understand what's happening in hard disk.
emphases.from_slide_quote: Bulk of secondary storage for modern computers is hard disk drives (HDDs) and nonvolatile memory (NVM) devices

emphases.statement: The physical construction of Hard Disk Drives, including platters, arm assembly, and read/write heads, directly dictates their operational principles and performance characteristics.
emphases.why: Understanding the physical structure is fundamental to comprehending *why* HDDs have particular performance limitations and design considerations, such as the need for scheduling algorithms. This serves LO3 by explaining the underlying mechanisms for efficient use.
emphases.evidence: The teacher describes each physical component in detail, linking its function to performance. Slides include a diagram of the 'Moving-head Disk Mechanism'.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: the hard disk actually has a structure of this guy. ...The first is the spinal plateters and the whole assembly rotates... and heads, magnetic heads...
emphases.from_slide_quote: HDDs spin platters of magnetically-coated material under moving read-write heads

emphases.statement: The concept of a 'cylinder' in HDD organization, where tracks of the same radius across multiple platters are grouped, is a key design mechanism to reduce the frequency and impact of arm assembly movement (seek time).
emphases.why: This is a specific design optimization to improve HDD performance. Grasping this concept demonstrates an understanding of the 'mechanisms developed' for efficient computer use (LO3) and how it impacts 'efficiency' (LO1).
emphases.evidence: The teacher introduces and explains the 'cylinder' concept as a way to read more data with fewer physical movements of the arm assembly. Slides mention cylinders in the context of address mapping.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: we would like to introduce a new concept. The equidistant or equicentric tracks for a cylinder. Why? Because when this one moves we would like to read or write as much data as possible because we don't want to make this movement very, very frequent.
emphases.from_slide_quote: The 1-dimensional array of logical blocks is mapped into the sectors of the disk sequentially... then the rest of the tracks in that cylinder

emphases.statement: The total I/O time for a hard disk operation is a sum of multiple distinct delays: positional (seek) delay, rotational delay (latency), data transfer time, and controller overhead, with positional delay often being the most significant.
emphases.why: Decomposing I/O time is crucial for performance analysis and optimization. It provides the framework for measuring efficiency (LO1) and understanding how different factors contribute to overall access time.
emphases.evidence: The teacher clearly lists and rephrases the components of total I/O time, emphasizing positional delay. Slide 7 explicitly presents the formula for 'Average I/O time'.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: So the overall time is the access time plus control over head plus transfer rate. Or let me just rephrase this. That is positional delay plus rotational delay plus transfer delay plus control over head for components of this.
emphases.from_slide_quote: Average I/O time = average access time + (amount to transfer / transfer rate) + controller overhead

emphases.statement: Performing a step-by-step calculation of average I/O time for a given data block is essential for concretely understanding how theoretical performance metrics translate into practical system delays.
emphases.why: This practical application is a direct way to 'measure the efficiency' (LO1) of computer resources. It's a common type of problem that tests a student's ability to combine definitions, formulas, and unit conversions.
emphases.evidence: The teacher dedicates over 11 minutes of the lecture to meticulously working through a numerical example calculation of I/O time. Slide 7 provides the same example with the final solution.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: So let us work on an example to do the computation for these components. So 4 kilobyte block... I need to sum up that one that one that one...
emphases.from_slide_quote: For example to transfer a 4KB block on a 7200 RPM disk... Average I/O time for 4KB block = 9.27ms + .031ms = 9.301ms

seed_quiz: Describe the physical components of a Hard Disk Drive and how they function together to read and write data.
Explain the three main components of I/O access time for an HDD (seek time, rotational latency, transfer time) and identify which one typically dominates.
Calculate the total average I/O time for a 2KB block transfer on a disk with 10,000 RPM, an average seek time of 4ms, a transfer rate of 800Mb/s, and a controller overhead of 0.05ms.
Compare and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of Solid State Drives (SSDs) versus Hard Disk Drives (HDDs) in terms of performance, cost, and lifespan.
What are Flash Translation Layer (FTL), garbage collection, and wear leveling, and why are these mechanisms necessary for efficient and durable Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) operation?
Describe the SCAN and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms, illustrating their head movements for a given queue of requests (e.g., 20, 100, 50, 180, 70, starting at 60 on a 0-199 cylinder disk).
Explain the concepts of error detection and error correction in the context of mass storage systems, providing an example of a technique for each.
How does a cylinder-based data organization improve HDD performance by reducing a specific type of delay?
What is 'write amplification' in NVM, and what potential impact does it have on an SSD's performance and lifespan?
Define low-level (physical) formatting, logical formatting, and disk partitioning in the context of storage device management.

learningOutcomes: Measure the efficiency in using the resources of a computer.
Evaluate the convenience in using the resources of a computer.
Explain the mechanisms developed between different components for the purpose of overall efficient use of a computer.

modules.loTitle: Measure the efficiency in using the resources of a computer.
modules.oneLineGist: Understand and calculate the total I/O access time for Hard Disk Drives by breaking it down into its core components.
coreIdeas: Total I/O Time = Seek Time + Rotational Latency + Transfer Time + Controller Overhead.
Seek Time: Time for the disk arm to move heads to the correct track/cylinder (dominant delay).
Rotational Latency: Time for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head (average is half a rotation).
Transfer Rate: Speed at which data flows between the drive and the computer.
Controller Overhead: Additional delay introduced by the disk controller.
mustRemember: The formula for Average Rotational Latency: (1 / (RPM / 60)) / 2.
Seek time is generally the largest component of I/O access time.
Unit conversions (milliseconds to seconds, KB to bits, Gb/s to bits/second) are crucial for accurate calculations.
A 'cylinder' is a set of tracks at the same radius across all platters, allowing multiple heads to access data without arm movement, thus improving efficiency.
modules.intuitiveExplanation: Imagine retrieving a specific book from a vast library. First, you need to walk to the correct aisle and shelf (that's your seek time – the longest part). Then, you wait for the specific book to become accessible on that shelf (rotational latency – it might be behind other books). Once it's there, you pick it up and carry it back (transfer time). And finally, there's a tiny bit of time for the librarian to log your request and give you the book (controller overhead). Total I/O time is the sum of all these delays.

examples.label: Calculating Total I/O Time (Lecture Example)
examples.description:
Transferring a 4KB block on a 7200 RPM disk with 5ms average seek time, 1Gb/s transfer rate, and 0.1ms controller overhead.
1. Seek Time = 5ms (given).
2. Rotational Latency: (1 / (7200/60)) / 2 = (1 / 120) / 2 = 0.008333s / 2 = 0.004166s = 4.17ms.
3. Transfer Time: 4KB = 32768 bits. 1Gb/s = 10^9 bits/s. Transfer Time = 32768 bits / (10^9 bits/s) = 0.000032768s = 0.033ms.
4. Controller Overhead = 0.1ms (given).
5. Total I/O Time = 5ms + 4.17ms + 0.033ms + 0.1ms = 9.303ms.

typicalQuestions: Calculate the total average time to read a 16KB block from an HDD given its RPM, average seek time, transfer rate, and controller overhead. Show your work and state assumptions.
A new HDD boasts 15,000 RPM. How does this impact its average rotational latency compared to a 7200 RPM disk? What other performance metric is often more significant?
Explain why the 'seek time' is typically the most dominating factor in HDD I/O performance.
commonTraps: Forgetting to convert RPM to revolutions per second before calculating latency.
Not dividing the full rotation time by 2 to get *average* rotational latency.
Incorrectly converting data size (KB) to bits for transfer rate calculations (e.g., mixing bits/bytes, MB/GB).
Overlooking controller overhead in the final calculation.

miniQuiz.question: If a disk rotates at 10,000 RPM, what is its average rotational latency in milliseconds?
miniQuiz.why: Full rotation time = 60s / 10,000 = 0.006s. Average rotational latency = 0.006s / 2 = 0.003s = 3ms.

miniQuiz.question: Which component of I/O time is typically the largest contributor to overall delay in an HDD?
miniQuiz.why: The mechanical movement of the read/write arm across the platters (seek time) takes significantly longer than rotational latency or data transfer for typical block sizes.

miniQuiz.question: What is the primary benefit of the 'cylinder' concept in HDDs for efficiency?
miniQuiz.why: By storing data belonging to the same file or logical block across tracks at the same radius (a cylinder), the system can access all data on that cylinder without moving the read/write arm, significantly reducing time-consuming seek operations.

modules.loTitle: Evaluate the convenience in using the resources of a computer.
modules.oneLineGist: Compare and contrast Hard Disk Drives (HDDs), Solid State Drives (SSDs), and Volatile Memory (RAM drives) based on performance, cost, reliability, and lifespan.
coreIdeas: HDDs offer high capacity and lower cost per MB, but are slower, have moving parts, and are susceptible to physical shock.
SSDs are significantly faster (no seek time or rotational latency), more reliable (no moving parts), but have higher cost per MB and a limited write lifespan.
Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) is a broad category including SSDs, USB drives, and surface-mounted flash memory.
RAM drives use volatile memory (DRAM) as mass storage, offering extreme speed but losing data on power loss and being expensive.
The 'convenience' of a storage device depends on the specific use case, balancing speed, capacity, cost, and durability requirements.
mustRemember: SSDs' key advantages: no mechanical delays (seek, rotational latency) and higher shock resistance.
SSDs' key disadvantages: higher cost per MB and finite write endurance (wear out over time).
RAM drives are the fastest but are volatile and generally expensive/limited capacity.
HDDs are still preferred for high-capacity, low-cost archival storage, especially in data centers.
modules.intuitiveExplanation: Choosing storage is like picking the right vehicle for a task. An HDD is a large, sturdy cargo truck: great for hauling lots of stuff cheaply, but it's slow and vulnerable to bumpy roads. An SSD is a speedy sports car: much faster, quieter, and more robust to bumps, but it costs a lot more per passenger and has a 'mileage limit' before it needs replacing. A RAM drive is like a rocket: incredibly fast for temporary tasks, but you can only carry a little bit, and if you turn it off, everything's gone!

examples.label: Laptop Storage
examples.description: A performance laptop might use an SSD for the operating system and applications (fast boot, quick app loading) and a larger, cheaper HDD for bulk data storage (photos, videos), leveraging the strengths of both.

examples.label: Data Centers
examples.description: Large data centers often use HDDs for their vast, cost-effective storage capacity for archival and less frequently accessed data, while employing SSDs for databases or frequently accessed 'hot' data that requires high IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second).

examples.label: Temporary High-Speed Cache
examples.description: RAM drives can be used in specialized applications (e.g., video editing, scientific simulations) where extremely fast temporary storage is needed for intermediate files, even though the data will be lost on reboot.

typicalQuestions: Compare and contrast the primary advantages and disadvantages of SSDs versus HDDs across reliability, performance, cost, and lifespan.
In what scenarios would an HDD still be a more convenient or cost-effective choice than an SSD?
Explain the concept of a 'RAM drive' and describe a specific use case where its unique characteristics make it the most convenient option.
commonTraps: Assuming SSDs are always 'better' without considering cost or write endurance.
Forgetting that RAM drives are volatile and data is lost on power off.
Not understanding *why* SSDs are faster (lack of mechanical parts vs. raw speed of flash memory).

miniQuiz.question: True or False: SSDs have 'seek time' and 'rotational latency' just like HDDs.
miniQuiz.why: SSDs are solid-state devices with no moving parts, thus they do not experience mechanical delays like seek time or rotational latency.

miniQuiz.question: What is 'DWPD' and what does it measure for NVM devices?
miniQuiz.answer: Drive Writes Per Day, it measures the expected lifespan/endurance of an SSD by indicating how many times the drive's full capacity can be written per day within its warranty period.
miniQuiz.why: DWPD is a critical metric for evaluating the lifespan and reliability of NVM devices, particularly SSDs, due to their finite write cycles.

miniQuiz.question: What is the main drawback of using a RAM drive for permanent storage?
miniQuiz.answer: It is volatile; data is lost when power is removed.
miniQuiz.why: RAM (DRAM) is volatile memory, meaning it requires continuous power to retain stored information. Once the system powers down, all data on a RAM drive is erased.

modules.loTitle: Explain the mechanisms developed between different components for the purpose of overall efficient use of a computer.
modules.oneLineGist: Identify and explain mechanisms like HDD cylinders, I/O scheduling, and NVM management algorithms (FTL, Garbage Collection, Wear Leveling) that ensure efficient and reliable storage use.
coreIdeas: Cylinder Concept (HDDs): A cylinder is a collection of tracks at the same radius across all platters. Reading/writing data within a cylinder avoids costly arm movement (seek time), improving efficiency.
I/O Scheduling: Algorithms (e.g., FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN) determine the order in which pending I/O requests are serviced to minimize head movement and improve throughput.
Flash Translation Layer (FTL): An abstraction layer in SSDs that maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical pages, enabling efficient data management and wear leveling.
Garbage Collection (NVM): A background process in SSDs that reclaims space from 'invalid' (deleted or old version) pages by consolidating valid data into new blocks, then erasing the old block.

Wear Leveling (NVM): An algorithm that distributes write/erase operations evenly across all physical memory blocks in an SSD to prevent premature wear-out of frequently written blocks, extending the device's lifespan.
Error Detection and Correction (EDC/ECC): Mechanisms (e.g., parity bits, checksums) implemented in hardware and software to detect and correct data corruption during storage and retrieval.
Swap Space: A portion of the hard disk or SSD used as a temporary extension of RAM (virtual memory) when physical memory is full, facilitating overall system operation.
mustRemember: The cylinder concept minimizes seek time on HDDs by grouping data on same-radius tracks.
NVM cannot overwrite pages in place; they must be erased in larger blocks, leading to the need for FTL and Garbage Collection.
Wear leveling is essential for extending the lifespan of SSDs by distributing write operations.
I/O scheduling algorithms aim to reduce disk head movement for better performance.
modules.intuitiveExplanation: Think of managing a busy warehouse. For HDDs, organizing items by 'cylinder' means putting related stuff on shelves reachable by the same robot arm position – so the arm doesn't have to crisscross the warehouse for every item. I/O scheduling is like a smart manager who groups incoming requests to minimize the robot's travel. For SSDs, it's trickier: you can't just 'update' an item on a shelf; you must move the entire shelf's contents, make changes, and put it on a *new* shelf (Garbage Collection). And because shelves wear out, you have a system (Wear Leveling) to ensure all shelves get roughly equal use, so none fail prematurely. The FTL is the master index that knows where everything *really* is, even after all the moving.

examples.label: HDD Cylinder Utilization
examples.description: When an operating system needs to store a large file, it tries to allocate disk blocks sequentially within a cylinder first. Once a cylinder is full, it moves to the next adjacent cylinder to continue storing the file, minimizing arm movement.

examples.label: SSD Write Operation
examples.description: When an application 'updates' a file on an SSD, the FTL directs the write to a new, empty physical page. The old page containing the original data is marked 'invalid'. Later, Garbage Collection will move any remaining valid data from that block and erase the entire block for reuse.

examples.label: Wear Leveling in SSDs
examples.description: If the SSD controller sees that a certain block has been written to many more times than others, it will deliberately route subsequent write requests to a less-used block, even if the FTL would logically point to the 'worn' block.

typicalQuestions: Describe the purpose of the 'cylinder' concept in HDDs and how it contributes to efficiency.
Explain the functions of the Flash Translation Layer (FTL), Garbage Collection, and Wear Leveling in Solid State Drives (SSDs). Why are these mechanisms necessary?
What is I/O scheduling, and how does it aim to improve the efficient use of computer resources in the context of mass storage?
commonTraps: Confusing the role of FTL with wear leveling; FTL maps addresses, wear leveling uses FTL to distribute writes.
Forgetting that NVM requires blocks to be erased *before* writing, unlike HDDs.
Misunderstanding that Garbage Collection is about reclaiming space from *invalid* pages, not just deleted files.
Overlooking the fundamental reason for NVM management challenges: the inability to overwrite in place and limited erase cycles.

miniQuiz.question: Why is 'wear leveling' crucial for the longevity of an SSD?
miniQuiz.answer: It distributes write/erase operations evenly across all physical memory blocks, preventing premature failure of frequently written blocks.
miniQuiz.why: NAND flash memory cells have a finite number of erase/write cycles. Without wear leveling, some blocks would wear out much faster than others, leading to early device failure.

miniQuiz.question: What is the primary goal of I/O scheduling algorithms for HDDs?
miniQuiz.answer: To minimize the total head movement and improve throughput.
miniQuiz.why: Head movement (seek time) is the slowest operation in an HDD. By ordering requests smartly, the OS can reduce unnecessary arm travel, making I/O faster.

miniQuiz.question: Which NVM management mechanism is responsible for mapping logical addresses to physical locations?
miniQuiz.why: The FTL acts as an intermediary, abstracting the complex physical layout and dynamic nature of flash memory from the operating system, allowing it to treat the SSD like a traditional block device.

cheatSheet.title: Mass-Storage Systems: HDD & NVM Cheat Sheet (Ch. 11)

sections.heading: 1. HDD: Enduring Relevance & Overview
bullets: **HDDs are still vital** for data centers, AI infrastructure, and high-capacity storage despite SSD growth.
Bulk of modern secondary storage: Hard Disk Drives (HDDs) and Nonvolatile Memory (NVM) devices.
Key focus: Understand physical structure, performance metrics, and I/O scheduling (LO1, LO3).

sections.heading: 2. HDD Physical Structure & Operation
bullets: **Platters**: Magnetically-coated disks, spin rapidly (e.g., 60-250 times/sec or 7200 RPM).
**Arm Assembly**: Monolithic structure moving Read/Write Heads across platters.
**Heads**: *Float* on an air cushion; physical contact with platter causes a 'disk crash' (damage).
**Cylinder**: Concept of all tracks at the same radius across *all* platters. Aims to minimize arm movement (seek time) for sequential reads/writes (LO3).

sections.heading: 3. Data Addressing & Organization
bullets: **Logical Blocks**: Disks addressed as a 1-dimensional array of logical blocks; smallest transfer unit.
**Mapping**: Logical blocks are sequentially mapped to physical sectors on the disk.
Mapping order: Sector 0 (first sector of outermost track) -> entire track -> rest of tracks in that cylinder -> next cylinder (outermost to innermost).
Sector: Physical storage unit; usually block size = sector size.

sections.heading: 4. HDD I/O Performance Factors (Delays)
bullets: **Positional Delay (Seek Time)**: Time for arm assembly to move heads to the desired track/cylinder. **Dominant delay for HDDs** (LO1, LO3).
**Rotational Delay (Latency)**: Time for the desired sector to rotate under the head. (Average = ½ of a full rotation time).
**Transfer Time**: Time to move the actual data between the drive and computer.
**Controller Overhead**: Time for the disk controller to process I/O requests.

sections.heading: 5. Calculating Total I/O Time
bullets: Total I/O Time = Average Access Time + (Amount to Transfer / Transfer Rate) + Controller Overhead (LO1).
Average Access Time = Average Seek Time + Average Rotational Latency.
Average seek time is often statistically measured as 1/3 of the maximum seek range.
**Crucial**: Meticulous unit conversions (KB to bits, milliseconds to seconds, RPM to rev/sec) for accurate calculations.

sections.heading: 6. Nonvolatile Memory (NVM / SSDs)
bullets: **SSDs**: NVM devices with no moving parts, thus **zero seek or rotational delay**. Much faster than HDDs.
More reliable, but typically higher cost per MB and potentially shorter lifespan due to write wear.
**Key Challenges**: Cannot overwrite data in-place; requires a larger 'block' erase operation first. Cells have limited erase cycles.
**Management**: Flash Translation Layer (FTL), Garbage Collection (GC), Wear Leveling (to distribute writes evenly across cells).

sections.heading: 7. Mass Storage Management & Scheduling
bullets: **Disk Attachment**: Storage accessed via I/O busses (e.g., SATA for HDD, NVMe for fast NVM direct to PCI bus).
**Controllers (HBAs)**: Special electronic processors that manage data transfers between device and computer DRAM via DMA.
**HDD Scheduling**: OS/device controller manages an I/O request queue to minimize seek time and maximize disk bandwidth (LO3).

formulas: Avg. Access Time = Avg. Seek Time + Avg. Rotational Latency
Total I/O Time = Avg. Access Time + (Amount to Transfer / Transfer Rate) + Controller Overhead
pitfalls: **Ignoring HDD's enduring relevance**: Still critical for data centers and cost-effective high-capacity needs.
**Neglecting Unit Conversions**: Essential for accurate I/O time calculations (e.g., bits vs. bytes, ms vs. s, RPM).
**Underestimating Seek Time**: Often the *largest* component of total HDD I/O time.
**Assuming SSDs function like HDDs**: SSDs have unique write characteristics (no in-place overwrite, limited write cycles).
**Misunderstanding 'Cylinder'**: It's a critical design mechanism to reduce expensive arm movements in HDDs.

quickQuiz.q: Why are HDDs still relevant today, despite SSDs?
quickQuiz.a: For data centers, AI infrastructure, and high-capacity, cost-effective bulk storage.

quickQuiz.q: What is the purpose of a 'cylinder' in HDD organization?
quickQuiz.a: To group equicentric tracks across all platters, minimizing arm movement (seek time) during sequential reads/writes.

quickQuiz.q: Which component typically dominates the total I/O time for a Hard Disk Drive?
Name two key challenges unique to Nonvolatile Memory (SSDs).
quickQuiz.a: Cannot overwrite data in-place (requires erase blocks), and cells have a limited number of erase cycles (wear).

quickQuiz.q: What is the smallest addressable unit of transfer on a disk?

# Address Mapping
 Disk drives are addressed as large 1-dimensional arrays of
logical
blocks
, where the logical block is the smallest unit of transfer
• Low-level formatting creates
logical
blocks
on physical media
 The 1-dimensional array of logical blocks is mapped into the sectors of
the disk sequentially
• Sector 0 is the first sector of the first track on the outermost
cylinder
• Mapping proceeds in order through that track, then the rest of the
tracks in that cylinder, and then through the rest of the cylinders
from outermost to innermost
• Logical to physical address should be easy

Except for bad sectors

Non-constant # of sectors per track via constant angular
velocity

where the head movement is reversed and servicing continues.

algorithm

elevator
algorithm

end of disk and those wait the longest

cylinder to the first one

 SSTF is common and has a natural appeal
 SCAN and C-SCAN perform better for systems that place a heavy load on the disk

## deadline
scheduler
• Maintains separate read and write queues, gives read priority

Because processes more likely to block on read than write
• Implements four queues: 2 x read and 2 x write

1 read and 1 write queue sorted in LBA order, essentially implementing
C-SCAN

1 read and 1 write queue sorted in FCFS order

All I/O requests sent in batch sorted in that queue’s order

After each batch, checks if any requests in FCFS older than configured
age (default 500ms)
–
If so, LBA queue containing that request is selected for next batch of
I/O
 In RHEL 7 also
NOOP
and
completely
fair
queueing
scheduler (
CFQ
) also
available, defaults vary by storage device

Ceph
determine where to store objects, manages
protection

storage cluster

scalable

# Background
 Code needs to be in memory to execute, but entire program rarely
used
• Error code, unusual routines, large data structures
 Entire program code not needed at same time
 Consider ability to execute partially-loaded program
• Program no longer constrained by limits of physical memory
• Each program takes less memory while running -> more programs
run at the same time

Increased CPU utilization and throughput with no increase in
response time or turnaround time
• Less I/O needed to load or swap programs into memory -> each
user program runs faster

--- Slide 5 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Virtual memory
 Virtual memory
– separation of user logical memory from physical
memory
• Only part of the program needs to be in memory for execution
• Logical address space can therefore be much larger than physical
address space
• Allows address spaces to be shared by several processes
• Allows for more efficient process creation
• More programs running concurrently
• Less I/O needed to load or swap processes

--- Slide 6 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Virtual memory  (Cont.)
 Virtual address space
– logical view of how process is stored in
memory
• Usually start at address 0, contiguous addresses until end of
space
• Meanwhile, physical memory organized in page frames
• MMU must map logical to physical
 Virtual memory can be implemented via:
• Demand paging
• Demand segmentation

# Virtual-address Space
 Usually design logical address space for
stack to start at Max logical address and
grow “down” while heap grows “up”
• Maximizes address space use
• Unused address space between
the two is hole

No physical memory needed
until heap or stack grows to a
given new page
 Enables
sparse
address spaces with
holes left for growth, dynamically linked
libraries, etc.
 System libraries shared via mapping into
virtual address space
 Shared memory by mapping pages read-
write into virtual address space
 Pages can be shared during
fork()
,
speeding process creation

# Basic Concepts
 With swapping, pager guesses which pages will be used before
swapping out again
 Instead, pager brings in only those pages into memory
 How to determine that set of pages?
• Need new MMU functionality to implement demand paging
 If pages needed are already
memory resident
• No difference from non demand-paging
 If page needed and not memory resident
• Need to detect and load the page into memory from storage

Without changing program behavior

Without programmer needing to change code

# Steps in Handling Page Fault
1.
If there is a reference to a page, first reference to that page will trap to
operating system
• Page fault
2.
Operating system looks at another table to decide:
• Invalid reference

abort
• Just not in memory
3.
Find free frame
4.
Swap page into frame via scheduled disk operation
5.
Reset tables to indicate page now in memory
Set validation bit =
v
6.
Restart the instruction that caused the page fault

# Aspects of Demand Paging
 Extreme case – start process with
no
pages in memory
• OS sets instruction pointer to first instruction of process, non-
memory-resident -> page fault
• And for every other process pages on first access
• Pure demand paging
 Actually, a given instruction could access multiple pages -> multiple
page faults
• Consider fetch and decode of instruction which adds 2 numbers
from memory and stores result back to memory
• Pain decreased because of
locality of reference
 Hardware support needed for demand paging
• Page table with valid / invalid bit
• Secondary memory (swap device with
swap space
)
• Instruction restart

# Instruction Restart
 Consider an instruction that could access several different locations
• Block move
• Auto increment/decrement location
• Restart the whole operation?

What if source and destination overlap?

# Free-Frame List
 When a page fault occurs, the operating system must bring the
desired page from secondary storage into main memory.
 Most operating systems maintain a
free-frame
list
-- a pool of free
frames for satisfying such requests.
 Operating system typically allocate free frames using a technique
known as
zero-fill-on-demand
-- the content of the frames zeroed-
out before being allocated.
 When a system starts up, all available memory is placed on the free-
frame list.

--- Slide 20 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Stages in Demand Paging – Worse Case
1.
Trap to the operating system
2.
Save the user registers and process state
3.
Determine that the interrupt was a page fault
4.
Check that the page reference was legal and determine the location of
the page on the disk
5.
Issue a read from the disk to a free frame:
a)
Wait in a queue for this device until the read request is serviced
b)
Wait for the device seek and/or latency time
c)
Begin the transfer of the page to a free frame

--- Slide 21 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Stages in Demand Paging  (Cont.)
6.
While waiting, allocate the CPU to some other user
7.
Receive an interrupt from the disk I/O subsystem (I/O   completed)
8.
Save the registers and process state for the other user
9.
Determine that the interrupt was from the disk
10.
Correct the page table and other tables to show page is now in memory
11.
Wait for the CPU to be allocated to this process again
12.
Restore the user registers, process state, and new page table, and then
resume the interrupted instruction

th

# Performance of Demand Paging
 Three major activities
• Service the interrupt – careful coding means just several hundred
instructions needed
• Read the page – lots of time
• Restart the process – again just a small amount of time
 Page Fault Rate 0

p

• if
p
= 0 no page faults
• if
p
= 1, every reference is a fault
 Effective Access Time (EAT)
EAT = (1 –
p
) x memory access
+
p
(page fault overhead
+ swap page out
+ swap page in )

--- Slide 23 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Demand Paging Example
 Memory access time = 200 nanoseconds
 Average page-fault service time = 8 milliseconds
 EAT = (1 – p) x 200 + p (8 milliseconds)
= (1 – p  x 200 + p x 8,000,000
= 200 + p x 7,999,800
 If one access out of 1,000 causes a page fault, then
EAT = 8.2 microseconds.
This is a slowdown by a factor of 40!!
 If want performance degradation < 10 percent
• 220 > 200 + 7,999,800 x p
20 > 7,999,800 x p
• p < .0000025
• < one page fault in every 400,000 memory accesses

--- Slide 24 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Demand Paging Optimizations
 Swap space I/O faster than file system I/O even if on the same device
• Swap allocated in larger chunks, less management needed than file
system
 Copy entire process image to swap space at process load time
• Then page in and out of swap space
• Used in older BSD Unix
 Demand page in from program binary on disk, but discard rather than paging
out when freeing frame
• Used in Solaris and current BSD
• Still need to write to swap space

Pages not associated with a file (like stack and heap) –
anonymous
memory

Pages modified in memory but not yet written back to the file system
 Mobile systems
• Typically don’t support swapping
• Instead, demand page from file system and reclaim read-only pages
(such as code)

--- Slide 25 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Copy-on-Write
 Copy-on-Write
(COW) allows both parent and child processes to initially
share
the same pages in memory
• If either process modifies a shared page, only then is the page copied
 COW allows more efficient process creation as only modified pages are copied
 In general, free pages are allocated from a
pool
of
zero-fill-on-demand
pages
• Pool should always have free frames for fast demand page execution

Don’t want to have to free a frame as well as other processing on page
fault
• Why zero-out a page before allocating it?
 vfork()
variation on
fork()
system call has parent suspend and child using
copy-on-write address space of parent
• Designed to have child call
exec()
• Very efficient

page it out

• Performance – want an algorithm which will result in minimum
number of page faults
 Same page may be brought into memory several times

of memory by modifying page-fault service
routine to include page replacement

modify
(

bit
to reduce overhead of page transfers – only

 Page replacement completes separation between logical memory and
physical memory – large virtual memory can be provided on a smaller
physical memory

# Basic Page Replacement
1.
Find the location of the desired page on disk
2.
Find a free frame:
- If there is a free frame, use it
- If there is no free frame, use a page replacement algorithm to
select a
victim frame
- Write victim frame to disk if dirty
3.
Bring  the desired page into the (newly) free frame; update the page
and frame tables
4.
Continue the process by restarting the instruction that caused the
trap
Note now potentially 2 page transfers for page fault – increasing EAT

# Page and Frame Replacement Algorithms
 Frame-allocation algorithm
determines
• How many frames to give each process
• Which frames to replace
 Page-replacement algorithm
• Want lowest page-fault rate on both first access and re-access
 Evaluate algorithm by running it on a particular string of memory
references (reference string) and computing the number of page faults
on that string
• String is just page numbers, not full addresses
• Repeated access to the same page does not cause a page fault
• Results depend on number of frames available
 In all our examples, the
reference string
of referenced page numbers
is
7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,0,3,2,1,2,0,1,7,0,1

# Least Recently Used (LRU) Algorithm
 Use past knowledge rather than future
 Replace page that has not been used in the most amount of time
 Associate time of last use with each page
 12 faults – better than FIFO but worse than OPT
 Generally good algorithm and frequently used
 But how to implement?

# LRU Algorithm (Cont.)
 Counter implementation
• Every page entry has a counter; every time page is referenced
through this entry, copy the clock into the counter
• When a page needs to be changed, look at the counters to find
smallest value

Search through table needed
 Stack implementation
• Keep a stack of page numbers in a double link form:
• Page referenced:

move it to the top

requires 6 pointers to be changed
• But each update more expensive
• No search for replacement

# LRU Approximation Algorithms
 LRU needs special hardware and still slow
 Reference bit
• With each page associate a bit, initially = 0
• When page is referenced bit set to 1
• Replace any with reference bit = 0 (if one exists)

We do not know the order, however

# LRU Approximation Algorithms (cont.)
 Second-chance algorithm
• Generally FIFO, plus hardware-provided reference bit
• Clock
replacement
• If page to be replaced has

Reference bit = 0 -> replace it

reference bit = 1 then:
–
set reference bit 0, leave page in memory
–
replace next page, subject to same rules

# Enhanced Second-Chance Algorithm
 Improve algorithm by using reference bit and modify bit (if available)
in concert
 Take ordered pair (reference, modify):
• (0, 0) neither recently used not modified – best page to replace
• (0, 1) not recently used but modified – not quite as good, must
write out before replacement
• (1, 0) recently used but clean – probably will be used again soon
• (1, 1) recently used and modified – probably will be used again
soon and need to write out before replacement
 When page replacement called for, use the clock scheme  but use the
four classes replace page in lowest non-empty class
• Might need to search circular queue several times

th

# Counting Algorithms
 Keep a counter of the number of references that have been made
to each page
• Not common
 Lease Frequently Used
(
LFU
)
Algorithm
:
• Replaces page with smallest count
 Most Frequently Used
(
MFU
)
Algorithm
:
• Based on the argument that the page with the smallest count
was probably just brought in and has yet to be used

# Page-Buffering Algorithms
 Keep a pool of free frames, always
• Then frame available when needed, not found at fault time
• Read page into free frame and select victim to evict and add to
free pool
• When convenient, evict victim
 Possibly, keep list of modified pages
• When backing store otherwise idle, write pages there and set to
non-dirty
 Possibly, keep free frame contents intact and note what is in them
• If referenced again before reused, no need to load contents again
from disk
• Generally useful to reduce penalty if wrong victim frame selected

# Applications and Page Replacement
 All of these algorithms have OS guessing about future page access
 Some applications have better knowledge – i.e. databases
 Memory intensive applications can cause double buffering
• OS keeps copy of page in memory as I/O buffer
• Application keeps page in memory for its own work
 Operating system can given direct access to the disk, getting out of
the way of the applications
• Raw disk
mode
 Bypasses buffering, locking, etc.

--- Slide 48 (Extracted) ---

# Allocation of Frames
 Each process needs
minimum
number of frames
 Example:  IBM 370 – 6 pages to handle SS MOVE instruction:
• instruction is 6 bytes, might span 2 pages
• 2 pages to handle
from
• 2 pages to handle
to
 Maximum
of course is total frames in the system
 Two major allocation schemes
• fixed allocation
• priority allocation
 Many variations

--- Slide 49 (Extracted) ---

th

# Fixed Allocation
 Equal allocation – For example, if there are 100 frames (after
allocating frames for the OS) and 5 processes, give each process 20
frames

# Global vs. Local Allocation
 Global replacement
– process selects a replacement frame from the
set of all frames; one process can take a frame from another
• But then process execution time can vary greatly
• But greater throughput so more common
 Local replacement
– each process selects from only its own set of
allocated frames
• More consistent per-process performance
• But possibly underutilized memory

threshold.
 This strategy attempts to ensure there is always sufficient free
memory to satisfy new requests

 Sometimes OS designers have a choice
• Especially if running on custom-built CPU
 Page size selection must take into consideration:
• Fragmentation
• Page table size
• Resolution

# TLB Reach
 TLB Reach - The amount of memory accessible from the TLB
 TLB Reach = (TLB Size) X (Page Size)
 Ideally, the working set of each process is stored in the TLB
• Otherwise there is a high degree of page faults
 Increase the Page Size
• This may lead to an increase in fragmentation as not all
applications require a large page size
 Provide Multiple Page Sizes
• This allows applications that require larger page sizes the
opportunity to use them without an increase in fragmentation

# Performance of Demand Paging
# 
## Stages in Demand Paging (worse case)
1.
Trap to the operating system
2.
Save the user registers and process state
3.
Determine that the interrupt was a page fault
4.
Check that the page reference was legal and determine the location of the page on the disk
5.
Issue a read from the disk to a free frame:
1.
Wait in a queue for this device until the read request is serviced
2.
Wait for the device seek and/or latency time
3.
Begin the transfer of the page to a free frame
6.
While waiting, allocate the CPU to some other user
7.
Receive an interrupt from the disk I/O subsystem (I/O completed)
8.
Save the registers and process state for the other user
9.
Determine that the interrupt was from the disk
10.
Correct the page table and other tables to show page is now in memory
11.
Wait for the CPU to be allocated to this process again
12.
Restore the user registers, process state, and new page table, and then resume the
interrupted instruction

# Memory Compression
 Memory compression
-- rather than paging out modified frames to swap
space, we compress several frames into a single frame, enabling the system to
reduce memory usage without resorting to swapping pages.
 Consider the following free-frame-list consisting of 6 frames
 Assume that this number of free frames falls below a certain threshold that
triggers page replacement.  The replacement algorithm (say, an LRU
approximation algorithm) selects four frames -- 15, 3, 35, and 26 to place on
the free-frame list. It first places these frames on a modified-frame list.
Typically, the modified-frame list would next be written to swap space, making
the frames available to the free-frame list.  An alternative strategy is to
compress a number of frames{\mdash}say, three{\mdash}and store their
compressed versions n a single page frame.

professorEmphases.statement: The step-by-step process of handling a page fault is a critical OS procedure that you must know.
professorEmphases.why: This is a classic operating systems exam question. Understanding this detailed sequence demonstrates a deep knowledge of how virtual memory is implemented.
professorEmphases.evidence: The slides dedicate three separate, detailed lists to this process (Slides 15, 20, 21), breaking it down into 6 and then 12 steps. This level of detail signals high importance.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: 1. If there is a reference to a page, first reference to that page will trap to operating system... 6. Restart the instruction that caused the page fault

professorEmphases.statement: Calculating Effective Access Time (EAT) is the key method to quantify the performance of a demand-paged system.
professorEmphases.why: This is a common quantitative problem on exams. You must be able to apply the formula correctly to show you understand the performance trade-offs of virtual memory.
professorEmphases.evidence: An entire slide (23) is dedicated to a specific numerical example of calculating EAT and interpreting the result, showing the dramatic slowdown caused by page faults. This focus on a calculation implies it's a testable skill.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: EAT = (1 – p) x memory access + p (page fault overhead + swap page out + swap page in )

professorEmphases.statement: The fundamental concept of virtual memory is the separation of the user's logical address space from the machine's physical memory.
professorEmphases.why: This is the foundational idea upon which the entire chapter is built. All other concepts, like demand paging and page replacement, are implementations of this core principle.
professorEmphases.evidence: The first few content slides (4, 5, 6) are dedicated to defining this concept and listing its many benefits (more programs running, less I/O, etc.), establishing it as the central theme.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: Virtual memory – separation of user logical memory from physical memory

professorEmphases.statement: The valid-invalid bit is the essential hardware mechanism that makes demand paging possible.
professorEmphases.why: Without understanding this bit, you cannot understand how the system detects that a page is not in memory and triggers the page fault trap. It's the switch that makes the whole system work.
professorEmphases.evidence: Slide 13 introduces the valid-invalid bit as the key component added to the page table, and the subsequent diagram (Slide 14) visually integrates it into the memory mapping process. Its function is central to the page fault mechanism.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: With each page table entry a valid–invalid bit is associated... During MMU address translation, if valid–invalid bit in page table entry is i  page fault

professorEmphases.statement: Page replacement is the necessary solution for when a page fault occurs but there are no free frames.
professorEmphases.why: This concept sets the stage for the next major topic in memory management. Understanding the *problem* (no free frames) is crucial before studying the *solutions* (FIFO, LRU, Optimal algorithms).
professorEmphases.evidence: Multiple slides at the end of the deck (28, 29, 31) are dedicated to setting up this problem, using direct questions like 'What Happens if There is no Free Frame?' to transition into this critical topic.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: Page replacement – find some page in memory, but not really in use, page it out

modules.title: Module 1: Fundamentals of Virtual Memory
modules.goal: To understand the core concepts of virtual memory, its benefits, and how it separates the logical view of memory from the physical hardware.

lessons.title: Lesson 1.1: The 'Why' of Virtual Memory
lessons.objective: Explain the motivation for virtual memory and list its key benefits, such as running programs larger than physical memory and increasing system concurrency.

activities.prompt: Review slides 4-6 on the background and definition of virtual memory.
activities.expected_outcome: You can summarize in your own words why virtual memory is a crucial OS concept and list at least three of its benefits.

activities.prompt: Write a short paragraph explaining the difference between logical memory (the user's view) and physical memory (the hardware reality).
activities.expected_outcome: A clear, concise distinction between the two memory concepts.

mini_quiz: What is the primary benefit of separating user logical memory from physical memory?
How does virtual memory help increase CPU utilization?

lessons.title: Lesson 1.2: The Virtual Address Space
lessons.objective: Describe the typical layout of a process's virtual address space and explain how it enables features like shared libraries and efficient process creation.

activities.prompt: Review slides 7-9, paying close attention to the diagrams of the virtual address space and shared libraries.
activities.expected_outcome: You can draw a simple diagram of a virtual address space, showing how the stack and heap grow.

activities.prompt: Find and watch a short (5-10 minute) video that animates how shared libraries are mapped into the virtual address space of multiple processes.
activities.expected_outcome: A visual understanding of how sharing memory saves physical resources.

mini_quiz: In a typical virtual address space layout, which direction does the stack grow?
How does virtual memory facilitate the sharing of system libraries between processes?

modules.title: Module 2: Demand Paging and Page Faults
modules.goal: To master the mechanics of demand paging, including the hardware support required and the precise sequence of events the OS follows to handle a page fault.

lessons.title: Lesson 2.1: Demand Paging Mechanics
lessons.objective: Explain how demand paging brings pages into memory only when needed, using the valid-invalid bit in the page table to trigger a page fault.

activities.prompt: Carefully study slides 10-14, focusing on the concepts of the 'lazy swapper' and the role of the valid-invalid bit.
activities.expected_outcome: You can explain the function of the valid-invalid bit and what it means for the bit to be 'v' versus 'i'.

activities.prompt: Draw a simplified diagram showing a logical address, a page table with valid-invalid bits, and physical memory. Show what happens when an address is referenced whose page table entry is marked 'invalid'.
activities.expected_outcome: A diagram illustrating the trigger for a page fault.

mini_quiz: What is a 'lazy swapper'?
What piece of hardware is responsible for checking the valid-invalid bit during address translation?

lessons.title: Lesson 2.2: The Page Fault Handling Procedure
lessons.objective: List and describe the step-by-step process the operating system executes to handle a page fault, from the initial trap to restarting the failed instruction.

activities.prompt: Memorize the steps listed on slides 15, 20, and 21. Understand what each step accomplishes.
activities.expected_outcome: You can write out the full sequence of events for a page fault from memory.

activities.prompt: Without looking at the slides, try to list the main stages of handling a page fault (e.g., Trap, Check, Find Frame, Swap in, Reset Table, Restart). Then, check your answer against the slides.
activities.expected_outcome: Accurate recall of the page fault handling sequence, a likely exam topic.

mini_quiz: What is the first thing the OS must do after a page fault trap occurs?
Why is it necessary to restart the instruction that caused the page fault?

modules.title: Module 3: Performance and Optimization
modules.goal: To analyze the performance impact of demand paging and understand optimization techniques like Copy-on-Write and the need for page replacement.

lessons.title: Lesson 3.1: Performance of Demand Paging
lessons.objective: Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT) given a memory access time, page fault rate, and page fault service time.

activities.prompt: Study slides 22-23 on the EAT formula and the example calculation.
activities.expected_outcome: You can write down the EAT formula and explain each of its components.

activities.prompt: Using the formula from slide 22, calculate the EAT if memory access time is 100ns, page-fault service time is 20ms, and the page fault rate is one in every 200,000 accesses.
activities.expected_outcome: A correct calculation demonstrating your understanding of the performance trade-offs.

mini_quiz: What does a high page fault rate do to the Effective Access Time?
Which component of the page fault service time takes the longest?

lessons.title: Lesson 3.2: Copy-on-Write and Page Replacement
lessons.objective: Explain how Copy-on-Write (COW) improves the efficiency of process creation and articulate why a page replacement algorithm is necessary when no free frames are available.

activities.prompt: Review slides 25-32, focusing on the diagrams for COW and the problem statement for page replacement.
activities.expected_outcome: You can explain COW in the context of a fork() system call and state the goal of a page replacement algorithm.

activities.prompt: Explain the role of the 'dirty bit' (modify bit) in the context of page replacement. How does it save time?
activities.expected_outcome: A clear explanation that using the dirty bit avoids unnecessary disk writes.

mini_quiz: Under what condition is a shared page actually copied when using Copy-on-Write?
What problem does page replacement solve?

emphases.statement: The fundamental concept of virtual memory is the separation of the user's logical address space from the machine's physical memory.
emphases.why: This is the foundational idea upon which the entire chapter is built. All other concepts, like demand paging and page replacement, are implementations of this core principle.
emphases.evidence: The first few content slides (4, 5, 6) are dedicated to defining this concept and listing its many benefits (more programs running, less I/O, etc.), establishing it as the central theme.
emphases.from_slide_quote: Virtual memory – separation of user logical memory from physical memory

emphases.statement: The valid-invalid bit is the essential hardware mechanism that makes demand paging possible.
emphases.why: Without understanding this bit, you cannot understand how the system detects that a page is not in memory and triggers the page fault trap. It's the switch that makes the whole system work.
emphases.evidence: Slide 13 introduces the valid-invalid bit as the key component added to the page table, and the subsequent diagram (Slide 14) visually integrates it into the memory mapping process. Its function is central to the page fault mechanism.
emphases.from_slide_quote: With each page table entry a valid–invalid bit is associated... During MMU address translation, if valid–invalid bit in page table entry is i  page fault

emphases.statement: Page replacement is the necessary solution for when a page fault occurs but there are no free frames.
emphases.why: This concept sets the stage for the next major topic in memory management. Understanding the *problem* (no free frames) is crucial before studying the *solutions* (FIFO, LRU, Optimal algorithms).
emphases.evidence: Multiple slides at the end of the deck (28, 29, 31) are dedicated to setting up this problem, using direct questions like 'What Happens if There is no Free Frame?' to transition into this critical topic.
emphases.from_slide_quote: Page replacement – find some page in memory, but not really in use, page it out

seed_quiz: A system has a memory access time of 150 nanoseconds and a page fault service time of 25 milliseconds. What is the maximum page fault rate (p) the system can tolerate if it wants a performance degradation of no more than 20%? Show your work using the Effective Access Time (EAT) formula.
List, in order, the 6 primary steps the operating system takes to handle a page fault, starting from the trap and ending with the instruction restart.
Explain the Copy-on-Write (COW) mechanism. How does it make the fork() system call more efficient?
Describe the role of the valid-invalid bit in a page table. What happens during an address translation if the bit is set to 'valid'? What happens if it is set to 'invalid'?
What are the three main benefits of implementing virtual memory in an operating system?

alignment.summary_chatty:
Since there's no lecture transcript, this analysis is based purely on the provided slides. The slides present a very logical and well-structured introduction to virtual memory. They start with the foundational 'why' (the benefits of separating logical and physical memory), then dive deep into the primary implementation mechanism, 'demand paging'. 

A significant portion of the slide deck is dedicated to the mechanics of handling a page fault, presenting the steps in detail, which indicates this is a high-priority topic likely to be on an exam. The slides also cover performance implications with a concrete example of calculating Effective Access Time (EAT), another strong signal of importance. The deck concludes by introducing advanced concepts like Copy-on-Write and setting up the problem of page replacement, which logically leads into the next lecture topic.

# Hard Disk Performance
 Access
Latency
=
Average
access
time
= average seek time +
average latency
• For fastest disk 3ms + 2ms = 5ms
• For slow disk 9ms + 5.56ms = 14.56ms
 Average I/O time = average access time + (amount to transfer /
transfer rate) + controller overhead
 For example to transfer a 4KB block on a 7200 RPM disk with a 5ms
average seek time, 1Gb/sec transfer rate with a .1ms controller
overhead =
• 5ms + 4.17ms + 0.1ms + transfer time =
• Transfer time = 4KB / 1Gb/s * 8Gb / GB * 1GB / 1024
KB = 32 /
(1024
) = 0.031 ms
• Average I/O time for 4KB block = 9.27ms + .031ms = 9.301ms

# Nonvolatile Memory Devices
 If disk-drive like, then called
solid-state
disks
(

)
 Other forms include
USB
drives
(thumb drive, flash drive), DRAM disk

 Can be more reliable than HDDs
 More expensive per MB
 Maybe have shorter life span – need careful management
 Less capacity

 Busses can be too slow -> connect directly to PCI for example

 Have characteristics that present
challenges
 Read and written in “page” increments

place

happen in larger ”block”
increments
• Can only be erased a limited
number of times before worn out –
~ 100,000

drive
writes
per

A 1TB NAND drive with rating

 Allocates
overprovisioning
to provide working space for GC

wear
leveling
needed to write equally to all
cells
NAND block with valid and invalid pages

# Disk Attachment
 Host-attached storage accessed through I/O ports talking to
I/O
busses

advanced
technology
attachment

),

,

SCSI

 Data transfers on a bus carried out by special electronic processors

# Address Mapping
 Disk drives are addressed as large 1-dimensional arrays of
logical
blocks
, where the logical block is the smallest unit of transfer
• Low-level formatting creates
logical
blocks
on physical media
 The 1-dimensional array of logical blocks is mapped into the sectors of
the disk sequentially
• Sector 0 is the first sector of the first track on the outermost
cylinder
• Mapping proceeds in order through that track, then the rest of the
tracks in that cylinder, and then through the rest of the cylinders
from outermost to innermost
• Logical to physical address should be easy

Except for bad sectors

Non-constant # of sectors per track via constant angular
velocity

--- Slide 16 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th

# HDD Scheduling
 The operating system is responsible for using hardware efficiently —
for the disk drives, this means having a fast access time and disk
bandwidth
 Minimize seek time
 Seek time

seek distance
 Disk
bandwidth
is the total number of bytes transferred, divided by
the total time between the first request for service and the completion
of the last transfer

--- Slide 17 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th

# Disk Scheduling (Cont.)
 There are many sources of disk I/O request
• OS
• System processes
• Users processes
 I/O request includes input or output mode, disk address, memory
address, number of sectors to transfer
 OS maintains queue of requests, per disk or device
 Idle disk can immediately work on I/O request, busy disk means work
must queue
• Optimization algorithms only make sense when a queue exists
 In the past, operating system responsible for queue management, disk
drive head scheduling
• Now, built into the storage devices, controllers
• Just provide LBAs, handle sorting of requests

Some of the algorithms they use described next

th

queue of I/O requests (of varying
“
depth
”
)
 Several algorithms exist to schedule the servicing of disk I/O requests
 The analysis is true for one or many platters
 We illustrate scheduling algorithms with a request queue (0-199)

# SCAN
 The disk arm starts at one end of the disk, and moves toward the
other end, servicing requests until it gets to the other end of the disk,
where the head movement is reversed and servicing continues.
 SCAN
algorithm
Sometimes called the
elevator
algorithm
 Illustration shows total head movement of 208 cylinders
 But note that if requests are uniformly dense, largest density at other
end of disk and those wait the longest

# C-SCAN
 Provides a more uniform wait time than SCAN
 The head moves from one end of the disk to the other, servicing
requests as it goes
• When it reaches the other end, however, it immediately returns to
the beginning of the disk, without servicing any requests on the
return trip
 Treats the cylinders as a circular list that wraps around from the last
cylinder to the first one

# Selecting a Disk-Scheduling Algorithm
 SSTF is common and has a natural appeal
 SCAN and C-SCAN perform better for systems that place a heavy load on the disk
• Less starvation, but still possible
 To avoid starvation Linux implements
## deadline
scheduler
• Maintains separate read and write queues, gives read priority

Because processes more likely to block on read than write
• Implements four queues: 2 x read and 2 x write

1 read and 1 write queue sorted in LBA order, essentially implementing
C-SCAN

1 read and 1 write queue sorted in FCFS order

All I/O requests sent in batch sorted in that queue’s order

After each batch, checks if any requests in FCFS older than configured
age (default 500ms)
–
If so, LBA queue containing that request is selected for next batch of
I/O
 In RHEL 7 also
NOOP
and
completely
fair
queueing
scheduler (
CFQ
) also
available, defaults vary by storage device

--- Slide 25 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# NVM Scheduling
 No disk heads or rotational latency but still room for optimization
 In RHEL 7
NOOP
(no scheduling) is used but adjacent LBA requests
are combined
• NVM best at random I/O, HDD at sequential
• Throughput can be similar
• Input/Output operations per second
(
IOPS
) much higher with
NVM (hundreds of thousands vs hundreds)
• But
write
amplification
(one write, causing garbage collection and
many read/writes) can decrease the performance advantage

# Error Detection and Correction
 Fundamental aspect of many parts of computing (memory, networking,
storage)
 Error
detection
determines if there a problem has occurred (for
example a bit flipping)
• If detected, can halt the operation
• Detection frequently done via parity bit
 Parity one form of
checksum
– uses modular arithmetic to compute,
store, compare values of fixed-length words
• Another error-detection method common in networking is
cyclic
redundancy
check
(
CRC
) which uses hash function to detect
multiple-bit errors
 Error-correction
code
(
ECC
) not only detects, but can correct some
errors
• Soft errors correctable, hard errors detected but not corrected

--- Slide 27 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th

# Storage Device Management
 Low-level
formatting
, or
physical
formatting
— Dividing a disk into
sectors that the disk controller can read and write
• Each sector can hold header information, plus data, plus error
correction code (
ECC
)
• Usually 512 bytes of data but can be selectable
 To use a disk to hold files, the operating system still needs to record its
own data structures on the disk
• Partition
the disk into one or more groups of cylinders, each
treated as a logical disk
• Logical
formatting
or
“
making a file system
”
• To increase efficiency most file systems group blocks into
clusters

Disk I/O done in blocks

File I/O done in clusters

--- Slide 28 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Storage Device Management (cont.)
 Root
partition
contains the OS, other partitions can hold other
Oses, other file systems, or be raw
• Mounted
at boot time
• Other partitions can mount automatically or manually
 At mount time, file system consistency checked
• Is all metadata correct?

If not, fix it, try again

If yes, add to mount table, allow access
 Boot block can point to boot volume or boot loader set of blocks that
contain enough code to know how to load the kernel from the file
system
• Or a boot management program for multi-os booting

--- Slide 29 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Device Storage Management (Cont.)
 Raw disk access for apps that
want to do their own block
management, keep OS out of the
way (databases for example)
 Boot block initializes system
• The bootstrap is stored in
ROM, firmware
• Bootstrap
loader
program
stored in boot blocks of boot
partition
 Methods such as
sector
sparing
used to handle bad blocks
Booting from secondary
storage in Windows

# Swap-Space Management
 Used for moving entire processes (swapping), or pages (paging),
from DRAM to secondary storage when DRAM not large enough for
all processes
 Operating system provides
swap
space
management
• Secondary storage slower than DRAM, so important to optimize
performance
• Usually multiple swap spaces possible – decreasing I/O load on
any given device
• Best to have dedicated devices
• Can be in raw partition or a file within a file system (for
convenience of adding)
• Data structures for swapping on Linux systems:

# Storage Attachment
 Computers access storage in three ways
• host-attached
• network-attached
• cloud
 Host attached access through local I/O ports, using one of several
technologies
• To attach many devices, use storage busses such as USB, firewire,
thunderbolt
• High-end systems use
fibre
channel
(
FC
)

High-speed serial architecture using fibre or copper cables

Multiple hosts and storage devices can connect to the FC fabric

--- Slide 32 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th

# Network-Attached Storage
 Network-attached storage (
NAS
) is storage made available over a
network rather than over a local connection (such as a bus)
• Remotely attaching to file systems
 NFS and CIFS are common protocols
 Implemented via remote procedure calls (RPCs) between host and
storage over typically TCP or UDP on IP network
 iSCSI
protocol uses IP network to carry the SCSI protocol
• Remotely attaching to devices (blocks)

# Cloud Storage
 Similar to NAS, provides access to storage across a network
• Unlike NAS, accessed over the Internet or a WAN to remote
data center
 NAS presented as just another file system, while cloud storage is
API based, with programs using the APIs to provide access
• Examples include Dropbox, Amazon S3, Microsoft OneDrive,
Apple iCloud
• Use APIs because of latency and failure scenarios (NAS
protocols wouldn’t work well)

--- Slide 34 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Storage Array
 Can just attach disks, or arrays of disks
 Avoids the NAS drawback of using network bandwidth
 Storage Array has controller(s), provides features to attached host(s)
• Ports to connect hosts to array
• Memory, controlling software (sometimes NVRAM, etc)
• A few to thousands of disks
• RAID, hot spares, hot swap (discussed later)
• Shared storage -> more efficiency
